title: Two weeks for a name
summary: The writer's friend has had a new cat for two weeks but has not given her a name. A short essay on why waiting can be the right thing.
body:
Last month my friend got a cat. She asked me to come and meet the cat. The cat is small, grey, and three months old.

I said, 'What is her name?'

My friend said, 'I have not decided.'

I asked, 'How long have you had her?'

She said, 'Two weeks.'

Many people choose a pet's name in the first hour. They look at the animal, they think of an old TV character, and they decide. Two weeks is unusual.

But my friend was not lazy. She was waiting. She wanted to see what the cat was like before she put a word on her. The first week, the cat was shy. The second week, the cat was loud. The third week, the cat was both.

In the end, my friend chose the name 'Misa.' Misa is from a Japanese book she likes. The name means 'long.' It is not because the cat is long. It is because the cat takes a long time to be still.

I have started to think we name things too fast. A cat with the wrong name is not a problem. But a name with a story is a better thing than a name without one. A name with a story means somebody waited.

My friend's cat answers to the name. The name fits.

grammarNotes.title: 'To + infinitive' for purpose
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'to + động từ nguyên thể' để chỉ mục đích — vì sao ai đó làm điều gì. Trong bài: 'She asked me to come and meet the cat.' (mục đích của lời mời) 'She wanted to see what the cat was like before she put a word on her.' (mục đích của việc chờ) 'The cat takes a long time to be still.' (mục đích / kết quả). 'To + V' thường đứng ngay sau động từ như 'come', 'want', 'try', 'take time'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'to + base verb' to show purpose — why someone did something. The passage: 'She asked me to come and meet the cat.' (purpose of the invitation) 'She wanted to see what the cat was like…' (purpose of waiting) 'The cat takes a long time to be still.' (purpose / result). 'To + verb' often follows verbs like 'come', 'want', 'try', 'take time'.

translationVi:
Tháng trước bạn tôi mua một con mèo. Cô mời tôi đến gặp con mèo. Con mèo nhỏ, xám, ba tháng tuổi.

Tôi hỏi: 'Tên gì?'

Bạn tôi nói: 'Tớ chưa quyết.'

Tôi hỏi: 'Cậu có nó bao lâu rồi?'

Cô đáp: 'Hai tuần.'

Nhiều người chọn tên thú cưng trong giờ đầu. Họ nhìn con vật, nghĩ đến một nhân vật trên TV cũ, rồi quyết. Hai tuần là bất thường.

Nhưng bạn tôi không lười. Cô đang đợi. Cô muốn xem con mèo như thế nào trước khi đặt một từ lên nó. Tuần đầu, mèo nhút nhát. Tuần hai, mèo ồn. Tuần ba, mèo cả hai.

Cuối cùng bạn tôi chọn tên 'Misa.' Misa từ một cuốn sách Nhật cô thích. Tên có nghĩa là 'dài'. Không phải vì mèo dài. Mà vì mèo cần một thời gian dài để đứng yên.

Tôi đã bắt đầu nghĩ chúng ta đặt tên cho mọi thứ quá nhanh. Một con mèo bị đặt sai tên không phải vấn đề. Nhưng một cái tên có câu chuyện thì tốt hơn một cái tên không có. Một cái tên có câu chuyện nghĩa là có người đã đợi.

Con mèo của bạn tôi đáp lại khi gọi tên. Cái tên hợp. Hai tuần là khoảng vừa đủ.

questions.prompt: How long has the friend had the cat by the time of the conversation?
Why has the friend not chosen a name?
options: She is waiting to see what the cat is like.
questions.explanation: 'She wanted to see what the cat was like before she put a word on her.'
questions.hint: Look at the writer's defence of the friend.

questions.prompt: How was the cat in each of the first three weeks?
options: First week shy; second week loud; third week both

questions.prompt: What name does the friend choose?
Where does the name come from?
options: A Japanese book the friend likes; the name means 'long.'
questions.explanation: 'Misa is from a Japanese book she likes. The name means "long."'

options: Because the cat takes a long time to be still — not because the cat is long.
questions.explanation: 'It is because the cat takes a long time to be still.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say about naming things?
options: We should name things in the first second.
questions.explanation: 'I have started to think we name things too fast.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe a name with a story?
options: Better than a name without one — a name with a story means somebody waited.
questions.explanation: 'A name with a story is a better thing than a name without one. A name with a story means somebody waited.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Waiting before you name something — a pet, a project, a child — can let the name carry a story that fast naming cannot.
Cats should be named after Japanese books.
Naming a pet is a waste of time.
questions.explanation: The writer defends the waiting and the resulting story.

blanks.explanation: A long time to be still.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says a name with a story means somebody waited. Is there a name in your own life — of a pet, a project, a child, even a habit — that you chose too quickly? What would the name have been if you had waited two weeks first?
